text: to be served. It is a quite astonishing development, considering the importance of such places for everyday sociability in a country like Italy. In fact, one would notice how an increasing number of persons entering the bar would not order anything to drink or eat at all, would not greet anyone with a ‘Good morning, how are you?’, as people normally do in Italy, but would instead go straight to the gambling machines and start dropping in coins. Maybe they did so because the real betting shops would only open later in the morning. By 2013 there were nine of those betting shops in my neighbourhood. This development is not confined to an anecdote from my neighbourhood; it is not confined to Rome, or Italy, or even Europe. It is happening everywhere and all the time, day and night. In Italy, as in many other European countries, gambling started to spread during the 1990s, in the general context of deregulation, 1 and the growth of the phenomenon has accompanied the spread of computers. I would sometimes wonder about this phenomenon as I made my way to the office. Turning on the computer, the wonder frequently turned into irritation, as offers of internet gambling kept popping up in small windows inside my Yahoo email account: ‘Win 1 million euro today!’. It has happened more than once that such windows would block my screen; when I subsequently, in perfect ignorance, clicked on the ‘close window’ symbol, I was instantly carried into one of those virtual gambling websites where everything is possible, or so they claim. Gambling practices have spread like a virus, penetrating all public and private spheres. Parents around the world have slowly started to worry, although, arguably, much less than we should. In Italy, where, as we shall see, modern gambling has its origin, it was attempted in 2012 at the political level to establish at least some minimum demarcations, prohibiting gambling houses and gambling machines in close proximity to schools, hospitals and churches. The attempt was in vain, as any such initiative was judged a violation of free trade and business. In several Italian cities, groups of married women started to protest and demand political control of the phenomenon: their husbands had all caught the ‘gambling bug’, and it was ruining their families. But it has all been to no avail. Gambling marches on, unstoppable. Most people reject permanent entry into this chaotic, passion- driven world and prefer to sample it only during brief visits to casinos or as they now and again click their way to (mis)fortune on the internet. But some people can’t resist gambling. They get caught up in endless gaming. It would be convenient if we could categorize such practices, and the people engaging them, as belonging to ‘deviant behaviour’ – as so much of the literature on gambling does with respect to gambling addicts. This, however, will not 1 The Italian state started to adopt new measures towards gambling from 1992. Italy risked default for its inability to service the public debt and the government recognized in gambling a fiscal policy measure. It helped the country into the Eurozone. Between 1992 and 2013, total gambling expenditure has increased approximately 800 per cent. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 143 really help us to capture what is going on. Our focus here is not on ‘problem gamblers’ or ‘addicts’; what we need to understand is not only how individuals lose control and must resort to medical treatment for their illness; the pathology is not (only) in that deviance – the pathology unfolds from within the heart of the very conditions of meaningful sociability. What is going on? Why is gambling spreading like wildfire? Where does it come from? And how are we to understand it? These are the questions we must discuss. While Victor Turner, in his essay, Liminal to Liminoid, did mention gambling, and also noticed its shared etymology with the notion of play (Turner 1982 [1974]), he never ventured into any analysis of it. This chapter, therefore, engages in a discussion concerning the spread of gaming and gambling in contemporary culture. The proliferation of gambling is one of the most evident ways in which liminal practices are moving to the core of everyday practices. Relatedly, and significantly, games are now proliferating among children who – and this is no minor detail – routinely carry around their own small ‘game- boxes’, enabling them to play anywhere they go. Many of these games that we let our children play at a still younger age are about killing; the player gains points for each time they manage to eliminate a person or a figure. They quickly become very skilled in their killing techniques. Game producers target children at a still younger age. The race to the bottom was (quite literally) reached when the ‘iPotty’ was introduced at the Consumer Electronics Show in early 2013, training children to deal with their natural functions by interacting with a gaming screen attached to the potty. Plato discussed the role of children’s play in a crucial passage in what is arguably the most crucial of all pieces, The Laws. He would not have hesitated one second to diagnose this phenomenon as a disease of soul undermining our political existence. In fact, the starting premise of Plato’s analysis is that life in the city has become utterly corrupted. The masses are being driven by the relentless pursuit of the pleasure principle, and have lost any distinction between what is good and bad. The populace of the polis has turned into a theatre-going audience waiting for the next ‘kick’ experience. It is this diagnosis of an emerging ‘theatrocracy’ that Szakolczai elaborates in full detail in his recent work (2013, see in particular pp. 30–33) on the genealogy of the public sphere.
